coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dot-modified electrode for monitoring of multivitamins in energy drinks
coredata.dc:creator: Martins, Eduardo Constante
Santana, Edson Roberto
Spinelli, Almir
coredata.dc:description: A glassy carbon electrode was modified with nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots immobilized in chitosan for the monitoring of multivitamins. The graphene quantum dots were synthesized using a simple citric acid/l-cysteine pyrolysis procedure. The co-doping with nitrogen and sulfur in the graphene matrix was confirmed by spectroscopic techniques. Electron microscopy results showed that the synthesized quantum dots had a diameter of 3.4 ± 1.4 nm. Electrochemical techniques showed excellent current responses to vitamin oxidation provided by the modified electrode compared to the bare electrode. The parameters of square wave voltammetry were optimized in order to obtain the best current responses and to study the electrochemical oxidation of vitamins. The calibration plots for vitamins B2, B6 and B12 were constructed in 0.1 mol L−1 sodium acetate buffer (pH 5.0) with limits of detection of 0.30, 30.1 and 0.32 nmol L−1, respectively. Lastly, the modified electrode was effectively implemented in the quantification of vitamins in classic and fruit-based energy drink samples.

Cyclic square wave voltammograms for (A) 1.0 μmol L−1 VB12 and 0.5 μmol L−1 VB2 and (B) 2.0 μmol L−1 VB6 at the (a) GCE, (b) CS/GCE and (c) N,S-GQD-CS/GCE in 0.1 mol L−1 B-R buffer (pH 5.0). SWV parameters: a = 50 mV, f = 50 Hz, and ΔEs = 1.0 mV.

Square wave voltammograms for (A, B) 1.0 μmol L−1 VB12 and 0.5 μmol L−1 VB2 and (C) 2.0 μmol L−1 VB6 at the N,S-GQD-CS/GCE in 0.1 mol L−1 B-R buffer at different pH values. SWV parameters: a = 50 mV, f = 50 Hz, and ΔEs = 1.0 mV.

(A) Square wave voltammograms obtained with the N,S-GQD-CS/GCE in 0.1 mol L−1 sodium acetate buffer (pH 5.0) for VB12 and VB2 at concentrations of: (a) blank, (b) 0.001, (c) 0.005, (d) 0.01, (e) 0.025, (f) 0.05, (g) 0.1, (h) 0.5, (i) 1.0, (j) 1.5, (k) 2.0, (l) 4.0, (m) 6.0, and (n) 8.0 μmol L−1. Inset: Zoom of (a)–(f) curves. SWV conditions: E = −0.90 V, t = 60 s, f = 60 Hz, ΔEs = 3.0 mV and a = 30 mV. (B) Square wave voltammograms for VB6 in the concentration range: (a) blank, (b) 0.1, (c) 0.5, (d) 1.0, (e) 2.0, (f) 4.0, (g) 6.0, (h) 8.0, (i) 10.0, (j) 12.0, (k) 14.0, (l) 16.0, and (m) 18.0 μmol L−1. SWV parameters: f = 70 Hz, ΔEs = 2.0 mV, and a = 30 mV.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dot-modified electrode for monitoring of multivitamins in energy drinks

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A glassy carbon electrode was modified with nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots immobilized in chitosan for the monitoring of multivitamins. The graphene quantum dots were synthesized using a simple citric acid/l-cysteine pyrolysis procedure. The co-doping with nitrogen and sulfur in the graphene matrix was confirmed by spectroscopic techniques. Electron microscopy results showed that the synthesized quantum dots had a diameter of 3.4 ± 1.4 nm. Electrochemical techniques showed excellent current responses to vitamin oxidation provided by the modified electrode compared to the bare electrode. The parameters of square wave voltammetry were optimized in order to obtain the best current responses and to study the electrochemical oxidation of vitamins. The calibration plots for vitamins B2, B6 and B12 were constructed in 0.1 mol L−1 sodium acetate buffer (pH 5.0) with limits of detection of 0.30, 30.1 and 0.32 nmol L−1, respectively. Lastly, the modified electrode was effectively implemented in the quantification of vitamins in classic and fruit-based energy drink samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Vitamins are a group of organic compounds that are vital for maintaining human health and, even in minute amounts, they play a significant role in healthy metabolism [1]. Vitamin B2 (VB2 – riboflavin), for example, serves as the essential metabolic precursor of many coenzymes, including flavin mononucleotide (FMN) and flavin adenine dinucleotide (FAD), which are involved in a myriad of enzymatic reactions, mainly associated with oxidative metabolism [2]. Vitamin B6 (VB6 – pyridoxine) acts as a cofactor for a large number of enzymatic reactions involved in amino acid, glucose and lipid metabolism and also in the synthesis of neurotransmitters and hemoglobin [3]. Vitamin B12 (VB12 – cobalamin) plays an essential role in maintaining neurological functions, in the production of blood cells, and provides a significant contribution to the synthesis of DNA [4]. Therefore, the determination of vitamins has gained widespread attention in recent years.

Various analytical methods for the quantification vitamins have been widely accepted, including chromatographic [5–7], fluorimetric [8,9], electrophoretic [10] and electrochemical [11–14] procedures. Electrochemical methods, in particular voltammetry, provide ease of operation, portability of the equipment and high sensitivity, which can be further improved with the use of modified electrodes [15,16]. These analytical tools can be obtained by immobilizing a variety of materials (inorganic and organic) on the substrate surface [17–20]. However, the simultaneous determination of vitamins using electrochemical sensors remains a challenge, with few studies being reported [1].

In recent years there have been reports of modified electrodes based on graphene quantum dots (GQDs). These materials consist of a few graphene sheets (smaller than 100 nm) and they exhibit excellent optical and electronic properties due to their quantum confinements and edge effects [21]. An interesting property of GQDs is that they offer countless possibilities for functionalization, which can enhance their attributes and applications [15,21]. In this regard, doping with heteroatoms, especially nitrogen and sulfur, is one of the most common approaches to introducing defects in the structure in order to modify the electron density and adapt the properties of GQDs [22,23]. Doping with heteroatoms generates permanent dipoles in the graphene structure, which provides greater surface reactivity and catalytic activity, facilitating adsorption and improving the performance of GQDs for their use in electrochemical devices [22,23]. Nitrogen and/or sulfur-doped GQDs are the most frequently investigated, mainly because nitrogen and carbon have similar atomic radii (N: 0.075 nm, C: 0.077 nm) while sulfur and carbon have similar electronegativity (S: 2.58, C: 2.55) [24]. These properties facilitate the incorporation of these heteroatoms into the graphene structure. However, the double doping of nitrogen and sulfur (co-doped) is still little explored [25].

In this report, we describe the development of an analytical method using a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) modified with nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots (N,S-GQDs) supported on a chitosan (CS) film for the simultaneous and sequential determination of vitamins B2, B6 and B12 in energy drinks. The N,S-GQDs were synthesized by a simple and fast pyrolysis method using citric acid and l-cysteine as precursors. CS was used in the preparation of the modified electrode in order to immobilize the N,S-GQDs in the matrix and allow the formation of a stable film on the GCE. The N,S-GQD-CS/GCE modified electrode was applied in the simultaneous determination of vitamins B12 and B2 and, in sequence, of vitamin B6 in energy drink samples.

A classic energy drink is defined as a non-alcoholic beverage that typically contains high levels of caffeine and other ingredients such as water, sugar, pigments, taurine, guarana extract, inositol, glucuronolactone, acidity regulator (e.g., citric acid or sodium citrate), and B-complex vitamins [26]. Recently, fruit-based energy drinks have been introduced, which can additionally contain polyphenolic compounds (provided by pineapple, strawberry, apple, pomegranate, orange, etc.) [26]. Energy drinks are very popular among adolescents and young adults, with a growing market that is estimated to reach $61 billion by 2021 worldwide [27]. Importantly, energy drinks have been reported to be associated with various health problems, such as headaches, arrhythmias, insomnia, and anxiety in adolescents [28]. A further relevant issue is the fact that energy drink consumption has been associated with negative lifestyles, such as poor dietary habits and substance use, especially alcohol [29].

Thus, as they are highly popular beverages, it is important to develop sensitive and fast analytical tools for use in performing quality control on these products, with regard to the quantification of vitamins. It should be noted that this study does not make any recommendation for the consumption of energy drinks, our objective is solely the development of an analytical method for determining the levels of vitamins in these beverages.

Chemicals were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich/Merck (Germany) and used without further purification. Citric acid anhydrous and l-cysteine were used for the synthesis of N,S-GQDs. The chitosan employed had a low molecular weight (75–85% deacetylated). The solutions were prepared with water purified in a Milli-Q system (Millipore, USA) with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ cm. Stock solutions of 1.0 mmol L−1 cyanocobalamin (VB12), riboflavin (VB2), pyridoxine hydrochloride (VB6) were prepared in ultrapure water. Four buffers were tested as the supporting electrolyte: Britton-Robinson (B-R) (H3BO3, CH3COOH, H3PO4), acetate buffer (CH3COOH, CH3COONa) phosphate buffer (NaH2PO4, Na2HPO4) and McIlvaine (citric acid, Na2HPO4). The pH adjustments were performed with 6.0 mol L−1 HCl or NaOH. Acetic acid (0.1 mol L−1) was used for the preparation of a 0.25% (w/v) chitosan solution. All solutions were stored under refrigeration at 5 °C.

The N,S-GQDs were obtained as described by Zhang et al. [30]. This one-step procedure involves pyrolysis with citric acid (CA) as a carbon source and l-cysteine as a sulfur and nitrogen double dopant. In a 5.0 mL beaker, 1.0 g of CA and 0.3 g of l-cysteine were mixed and heated to 240 °C for 4 min using a hotplate. The liquid obtained was then dissolved in 10.0 mL ultrapure water with the aid of an ultrasonic bath. The dispersion of N,S-GQDs was neutralized with 6.0 mol L−1 NaOH and made up to 50.0 mL with ultrapure water.

For the characterization studies, the N,S-GQD dispersion was diluted 100 X. The N,S-GQDs were characterized by attenuated total reflectance Fourier transform infrared (ATR FT-IR) spectroscopy using a Spectrum-100 spectrometer (PerkinElmer, USA) in the range of 550–4000 cm−1. The N,S-GQD dispersion was frozen and subsequently lyophilized for the ATR FT-IR studies. A UV-1800 ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) and a quartz cell (with a 1.0 cm optical path) were used for the optical characterization of the N,S-GQD dispersion. The characterization of the N,S-GQD dispersion by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was registered using a transmission electron microscope, model JEM-1011 (JEOL, Japan), operating at 100 kV. For the TEM analysis, an aliquot of 5.0 μL of the N,S-GQD dispersion was dripped onto a millimetric carbon-coated copper grid (300 mesh), which was kept for 24 h at room temperature for complete drying.

To prepare the modified electrode, 200 μL of the N,S-GQD dispersion was added to 9.8 mL of a 0.25% (m/v) chitosan solution and kept at 60 °C for 30 min under stirring. In the next step, 2.0 μL of the N,S-GQD-CS dispersion was dripped onto the GCE surface. For this procedure, the GCE was previously polished with aluminum oxide (0.05 μm), washed and subjected to an ultrasonic bath for 3 min in ultrapure water. The N,S-GQD-CS/GCE modified electrode was allowed to dry in an oven at 40 °C.

The electrochemical experiments were carried out on a portable potentiostat PalmSens 4 (Palm Instruments BV, The Netherlands). A cell containing three electrodes was used in the experiments: N,S-GQD-CS/GCE as the working electrode (geometric area = 0.07 cm2), Ag/AgCl (KCl saturated) as the reference electrode, and a platinum wire as the counter electrode.

Voltammetric analysis was performed simultaneously for VB12 and VB2 and sequentially for VB6. In this procedure, aliquots of standard solutions of VB12, VB2 and VB6 were first added to the electrochemical cell containing the deaerated supporting electrolyte after N2 purging (10 min). The optimized conditions for the simultaneous analysis of VB12 and VB2 by square wave anodic stripping voltammetry (SWASV) were pre-concentration potential −0.90 V, pre-concentration time 60 s under agitation, equilibration time 30 s, and anodic scan in the range of −1.0 to +0.1 V with frequency 60 Hz, potential step 3.0 mV and amplitude 30 mV. The analysis of VB6 was then performed by square wave voltammetry (SWV) with anodic scan from +0.4 to +1.3 V with the parameters frequency 70 Hz, potential step 2.0 mV and amplitude 30 mV.

The same potentiostat was used for the characterization of the N,S-GQD-CS/GCE by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The EIS spectra were recorded at open circuit potential using a 5.0 mmol L−1 equimolar mixture of K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] in 0.1 mol L−1 KCl. An amplitude value of 5.0 mV and a frequency range of 0.1–50,000 Hz were applied.

The N,S-GQD-CS/GCE was applied in the quantification of VB2, VB6, and VB12 in energy drinks. Classic and fruit-based energy drinks (A – Red Bull do Brasil Ltda, Brazil, B – Monster Energy Company, Brazil) were purchased in stores in Florianópolis, Brazil. The samples were transferred to beakers and subjected to an ultrasonic bath for 10 min for the complete removal of carbon dioxide. For sample A, an aliquot of 4.2 mL of degassed sample was then added to the electrochemical cell with 5.8 mL of supporting electrolyte for the voltammetric analysis. Sample B was analyzed with the same procedure, but using an aliquot of 3.0 mL of sample and 7.0 mL of supporting electrolyte in the electrochemical cell.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of N,S-GQDs and modified electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: The ATR-FTIR spectra of the N,S-GQDs and their precursors are given in Fig. 1 A. Both citric acid (curve a) and l-cysteine (curve b) are low-cost chemicals and rich in multifunctional groups (−OH, CO, –SH, –NH2), providing both carbon and nitrogen/sulfur sources for the formation of N,S-GQDs in a simple one-pot pyrolysis method [30]. The spectrum for the N,S-GQDs (curve c) presents broad absorption bands at 3450 and 2960 cm−1 characteristic of O–H and N–H stretching vibrations, respectively [31]. The double doping of the graphene structure was validated with a broad absorption band at 2560 cm−1, which was attributed to the stretching vibration of S–H, and with bands at around 1400 and 1180 cm−1, associated with the vibrations of C–N and C–S bonds, respectively [32].

Fig. 1B shows the UV–vis absorption spectra for citric acid (curve a) and l-cysteine (curve b) (precursors) in which no absorption bands were present, while in the spectrum for the N,S-GQDs (curve c) there was a shoulder peak at 250 nm and a well-defined absorption band at 350 nm. The absorption band at 250 nm can be ascribed to the π−π* transitions of the aromatic CC domains and the absorption band at 350 nm is ascribed to the n–π* transition of CO [30,33]. In addition, the N,S-GQDs emit strong bright blue fluorescence after the irradiation of UV light at 365 nm (inset Fig. 1B – cuvette c), indicating excellent photoluminescence for optical sensing.

The TEM image (Fig. 1C) shows that the synthesized N,S-GQDs were uniform and well dispersed. The transparent structures recorded in the image may be associated with N,S-GQDs consisting of a few layers of graphene, while the darker forms relate to structures composed of more layers of graphene [21]. The mean diameter of the N,S-GQDs was estimated from the darker structures (such as those highlighted by red circles in the image) to be around 3.4 ± 1.4 nm (inset Fig. 1C), which is consistent with the original study in which N,S-GQDs were synthesized through the pyrolysis of citric acid and l-cysteine [30].

The CV and EIS techniques using the [Fe(CN)6]3–/4– redox probe were applied to verify the better performance of the N,S-GQD-CS/GCE compared to the bare GCE. As N,S-GQDs are water-soluble, it was not possible to build an electrode with them alone, since there was no formation of a stable film on the substrate. Regarding the results obtained by CV (Fig. S1A), the current responses (anodic and cathodic) for the N,S-GQD-CS/GCE (curve c) were around twice as high as those for the CS/GCE (curve b) and approximately six times higher than responses obtained with the bare GCE (curve a). The superior performance of the CS/GCE can be attributed to the affinity of the positively-charged chitosan with the negative charges of the pair [Fe(CN)6]3–/4–, contributing to the adsorption of the species on the electrode surface [15,21]. Lastly, when N,S-GQDs are immobilized in chitosan, there is an increase in the current, which is also associated with an improvement in the conductivity and higher surface area provided by the N,S-GQDs [15,21]. The obtained peak-to-peak separations (ΔEp) were 75, 115 and 105 mV for GCE CS/GCE and N,S-GQD-CS/GCE, respectively. The increase in the value of ΔEp indicates greater difficulty associated with the electron transfer on the surface of the electrode, and consequently, the reaction is less reversible [34]. This behavior can be attributed to the non-conducting character of chitosan, and the semi-conducting character of N,S-GQDs. However, this result does not interfere with the proposed application in terms of the modified electrode performance or signal resolution.

To complement the electrochemical characterization, the increase in the electroactive area of the electrodes due to the modification with N,S-GQDs and CS was estimated through the Randles-Ševčík equation [34]. For this, cyclic voltammograms were recorded varying the scan rate from 10 to 100 mV s−1 using bare GCE (Fig. S1B), CS/GCE (Fig. S1C) and N,S-GQD-CS/GCE (Fig. S1D). The increase obtained in the value of the slope of the Ipa and ν ½ plot (inserted in Figs. S1B–D) for the different modification steps, indicated an increase in the electroactive area of the electrode since the other parameters of the Randles-Ševčík equation remained constant. Thus, the modification with the chitosan film provided an increase of 1.2 times in the electroactive area in relation to the bare GCE. While with the modification with the dispersion of N,S-GQDs-CS, the estimated increase was approximately 2.5 times compared to the bare GCE. The results indicated that CS, and principally the N,S-GQDs, contributed to the increase in the electroactive area of the modified electrode, which also causes an enhancement in sensitivity in electrochemical measurements.

More evidence of an improvement in the electrode performance following the modification of the surface with N,S-GQD-CS was obtained through data on the resistance to charge transfer (Rct) obtained in the EIS study. The Rct values fitted from the Nyquist plots (Fig. S2A) were 550, 435 and 91 Ω for GCE, CS/GCE and N,S-GQD-CS/GCE, respectively. The decrease in the Rct values demonstrated that electron transfer was favored between the [Fe(CN)6]3–/4– probe and the electrode interface. The electrostatic interaction between the positively charged chitosan and the negatively charged [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− probe could be responsible for the improvement in electron transfer observed for the CS/GCE, since chitosan is considered as a non-conductive polymer [15]. Finally, the most effective contribution to the improvement in electron transfer in the system was the presence of N,S-GQDs. The Rct value provided by the N,S-GQD-CS/GCE was the lowest of the electrodes tested, indicating that the presence of N,S-GQDs can significantly improve the conductivity and facilitate the electron transfer between the redox probe and the electrode interface. From the Bode plots, it can be seen that the modification of electrodes resulted in a decrease in impedance values (Fig. S2B). The impedance decrease might have arisen from the change in conductivity with the immobilization of N,S-GQDs in the electrode and electrostatic interaction with chitosan. In addition, the Bode plot of phase shift with the logarithm of frequency taken during the stage of modifications of electrodes showed that the maxima in the phase shifted to a lower frequency region as a result of the modification with CS and N,S-GQDs (Fig. S2C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behavior of vitamins B2, B6 and B12
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical behavior of the vitamins B2, B6 and B12 at different electrodes was investigated by cyclic square wave voltammetry (C-SWV). This technique combines square wave scans in the forward (anodic scan) and reverse (cathodic scan) directions in order to provide qualitative and quantitative information on electroactive species at low concentrations, for which cyclic voltammetry is often not sensitive enough [35]. The voltammograms of 1.0 μmol L−1 VB12 and 0.5 μmol L−1 VB2 (Fig. 2 A) were obtained by applying a potential variation of −1.0 to 0.0 V (forward) and 0.0 to −1.0 V (reverse). In sequence, for the study of 2.0 μmol L−1 VB6 (Fig. 2B) data was recorded from +0.6 to +1.2 V (forward) and +1.2 to +0.6 V (reverse).

The cyclic square wave voltammograms obtained for VB12 and VB2 with all electrodes tested show oxidation and reduction peaks for both species (Fig. 2A). The scans recorded in the two directions are similar, providing very close current and peak potential values, which characterizes a reversible reaction system [36]. The diagnosis of a reversible system corroborates cyclic voltammetry studies on VB12, in which the redox signals detected refer to the reversible reaction of Co(I)/Co(II) present in the center of the vitamin structure [13]. However, this electrochemical behavior cannot be associated with VB2, as its mechanistic evaluation by cyclic voltammetry reported in the literature describes a quasi-reversible process [37]. According to the literature, two mechanisms are possible for VB2 oxidation in an aqueous medium. In one scenario, oxidation occurs in two steps: a first electron is transferred in a quasi-reversible process to form a semi-quinone followed by a second electron transfer to form the fully oxidized flavine [38]. The other proposition states that the electrooxidation takes place in just one step via a two-electron and two-proton transfer process, showing only one oxidation peak [39]. The solution properties (e.g., pH) and the SWV parameters determine whether the reaction proceeds through the semi-quinone radical in two separate one-electron processes or via a two-electron and two-proton process [38,39]. The first mechanism appears to occur in this study, since it is possible to observe a shoulder in the oxidation peak obtained from the vitamin.

Regarding the electrochemical behavior of VB6, the voltammograms showed only one oxidation peak in the forward direction (Fig. 2B). This process refers to the primary alcohol group present in the structure of VB6 (pyridoxine), which undergoes oxidation with the release of two protons and two electrons, originating the corresponding aldehyde (pyridoxal) [40]. No significant reduction peak was recorded, which characterizes an irreversible system. This diagnosis is in agreement with the results of cyclic voltammetry studies published in the literature [41].

With regard to the electrode modification process, there were no significant shifts in the peak potentials of the vitamins, only improvements in the current intensities. In this respect, for all vitamins, the lowest current values were obtained using the bare GCE (curve a). With the CS/GCE electrode (curve b), the current gain for all species analyzed can be related to better adsorption of the analytes in the CS film. Lastly, with the N,S-GQD-CS/GCE (curve c), the current intensities were around five times higher than those obtained with the bare GCE. This improvement may be associated with the co-doping with heteroatoms in the structure of the graphene sheets, which improves the conductivity of the material, and also an elevated electroactive area provided to the electrode with the immobilization of this type of material. Thus, the N,S-GQD-CS/GCE electrode proved to be promising for the determination of vitamins B2, B6 and B12. For the subsequent development of a sensitive analytical method, only studies with SWV and the forward scan were conducted.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH and composition of supporting electrolyte
ce:section.ce:para: The influence of the pH of the supporting electrolyte on the electrochemistry of the vitamins was examined in the pH range of 2.0–12.0 for VB2 and VB12 (Fig. 3 A and B), and in the pH range of 2.0–7.0 for VB6 (Fig. 3C). The peak potentials for the vitamins shifted towards more negative values with slopes of −50.2 and −55.8 mV pH−1 for VB12 and VB2 (Fig. S3A), respectively, and −64.1 mV pH−1 for VB6 (Fig. S3B) as the pH of the medium increased. The proximity of these values with the value of −59.2 mV pH−1 (Nernst equation theoretical coefficient) indicates the transfer of the same number of mol of electrons and protons in each reaction. Regarding the current responses (Fig. S3C), the intensities increased from pH 2.0 to 4.0 for VB12 and VB2, and from 2.0 to 5.0 for VB6. As the pH becomes alkaline, the currents for all vitamins decreased. This behavior may be related to the instability of these vitamins at alkaline pH [42]. In addition, in the case of VB2, the alkaline pH of the medium contributes to the stabilization of the semi-quinone intermediate, causing the oxidation reaction of VB2 to occur in two steps and leading to the appearance of two oxidation peaks (Fig. 3B) [39]. In order to obtain a high sensitivity for all species, the pH value of 5.0 was selected for the subsequent studies (Fig. S3C).

The pH values of 4.0, 5.0, and 6.0 were then used to evaluate the influence of different compositions of supporting electrolytes on the electrochemical response to vitamins. The buffers tested were B-R, acetate, phosphate and McIlvaine, all at a concentration of 0.1 mol L−1. The highest current values were obtained in acetate buffer at pH 5.0 (Fig. S4). Therefore, this buffered solution was used in the subsequent analytical experiments.

The SWV parameters f, ΔEs and a were optimized in order to obtain the highest peak current intensities, as well as mechanistic information on the vitamin reactions. The f value was varied from 10 to 100 Hz for the simultaneous study of VB12 and VB2 (Fig. S5A) and then VB6 (Fig. S5B). For the development of a sensitive method, the values of 60 Hz for VB12 and VB2 (Fig. S5C) and 70 Hz for VB6 (Fig. S5D) were selected, as these were the conditions that provided the highest current responses.

In addition, the data extracted from the study of the frequency variation allowed a diagnosis of the reaction mechanism. In this regard, the linear relationship observed in the current vs. f
½ plot (Fig. S5E) indicates a reversible system for the reaction of VB12, in agreement with the results in Section 3.2. In addition, through the value of the slope of the peak potential vs. log f (Fig. S5F), which, according to SWV theory, corresponds to 2.3RT/2zF, it was possible to calculate the number of electrons (z) transferred in the reaction [43]. Thus, applying R, T and F values of 8.314 J mol−1 K−1, 298.15 K and 96485 C, respectively, and the slope of the graph (0.028), the z value obtained was close to 1. With this result and based on the conclusions in Section 3.3, it appears that the oxidation reaction of VB12 on the N,S-GQD-CS/GCE occurred through the transfer of one mol of electrons and one mol of protons for each mol of VB12. For VB6, the linear relationship observed in the current vs. f plot (Fig. S5G) corroborates the diagnostic of an irreversible system (also in Section 3.2). Furthermore, following the SWV theory for an irreversible system, through the value of the slope of the peak potential vs. log f (Fig. S5H), which corresponds to 2.3RT/zFα, it was possible to calculate the number of electrons transferred (z) in the reaction [43]. Thus, applying the values of R, T and F mentioned above, α = 0.5 (for an irreversible system), and the slope of the plot (0.056), a z value of close to 2 was obtained. Based on this data and the conclusions of Section 3.3, it appears that the oxidation reaction of VB6 on the N,S-GQD-CS/GCE occurred through the transfer of two mol of electrons and two mol of protons for each mol of VB6. Lastly, for VB2, there was no linear relationship between current and f (data not shown), which corresponds to a quasi-reversible electrochemical system. Furthermore, there is no theory available to calculate the number of electrons in a quasi-reversible system by SWV [43].

Next, the value of ΔEs was optimized. This value is responsible for the effective velocity of the SWV together with the f (v = fΔEs) [43] and the values that provided the greatest increase in the peak current intensities were selected. These values were 3.0 mV for VB12 and VB2 (Fig. S6A) and 2.0 mV for VB6 (Fig. S6B). Lastly, the influence of amplitude was evaluated, a parameter that affects not only the current intensity but also the peak resolution. A value of 30 mV was selected for the three vitamins (Figs. S6C and S6D), as this condition provided a significant increase in the peak current intensities, without causing a broadening of the peaks. Thus, the optimized SWV parameters for the simultaneous determination of VB2 and VB12 were f = 60 Hz, ΔEs = 3.0 mV and a = 30 mV and for the subsequent determination of VB6 they were f = 70 Hz, ΔEs = 2.0 mV, and a = 30 mV.

In order to obtain high sensitivity in the electrochemical method, a pre-concentration step was applied before the anodic scan. Initially, the application of pre-concentration potentials from −1.0 V to −0.80 V for 30 s for the simultaneous analysis of VB12 and VB2, and sequentially for VB6, were evaluated. The application of −0.90 V provided the highest current responses for the determination of VB2 and VB12 (Fig. S7A). This phenomenon is explained by the pre-concentration of VB12 as Co(I) and of VB2 in its reduced form on the electrode surface and posterior oxidation by SWV [44,45]. In the next step, pre-concentration times of 30–120 s were evaluated, with the highest current intensities obtained after 60 s for VB2 and VB12 (Fig. S7B). For VB6, there were no significant changes with the application of the pre-concentration step (data not shown).

Using the optimized experimental conditions, the square wave voltammograms showed well-defined peaks at −0.77 V and −0.31 V (vs. Ag/AgCl, KCl(sat)), corresponding to the oxidation of VB12 and VB2, respectively (Fig. 4 A), and a defined peak at +0.91 V attributed to the oxidation of VB6 (Fig. 4B). The current intensities increased proportionally with an increase in the concentrations of VB12 and VB2 in the range of 0.001–8.0 μmol L−1, and for VB6 in the range of 0.1–18.0 μmol L−1.

The linear regression for VB12 (Fig. S8A) can be expressed according to the function: ISW/μA = 17.55 (±0.03)[VB12] + 0.40 (±1.90 × 10−3) (r = 0.999), where ISW is the resulting peak current in μA and [VB12] is the concentration of VB12 in μmol L−1. The procedure used to determine the limits of detection (LOD) and quantification (LOQ) was based on the standard deviation (SD) of the intercept and the slope of the calibration plot (s), applying the formulas LOD = 3 × (SD/s) and LOQ = 10 × (SD/s) [46]. The LOD and LOQ values obtained for VB12 were 0.32 and 1.08 nmol L−1, respectively. Similarly, for VB2 (Fig. S8B), the linear equation can be expressed according to the function: ISW = 35.34 (±0.08)[VB2] − 0.03(±3.56 × 10−3) (r = 0.997). The LOD and LOQ values obtained for VB2 were 0.30 and 1.01 nmol L−1, respectively. For the VB6 (Fig. S8C), the linear equation can be expressed as: ISW = 0.94 (±0.02)[VB6] − 0.12(±9.45 × 10−3) (r = 0.997). The LOD and LOQ values obtained for VB6 were 30.1 and 100.1 nmol L−1, respectively.

Despite the considerable progress made in recent years in the field of electrode development for vitamin analysis, there are still few reports of electrochemical sensors for the simultaneous monitoring of vitamins [1]. Table 1 shows some studies on the simultaneous determination of vitamins by voltammetric techniques. These studies involved the simultaneous determination of VB2 and VB6, but studies that included VB12 could not be found. Thus, among the advantages of this study is the fact that the three vitamins were determined with detection limits much lower than those previously reported (Table 1).

The LOD values for the N,S-GQD-CS/GCE are also lower than the limits reported for chromatographic methods. Vállez-Gomis et al. [6] obtained LOD values of 236, 398, 827 nmol L−1 for VB12, VB2 and VB6, respectively, using liquid chromatography with ultraviolet detection (LC-UV). Similarly, Sasaki et al. [7] obtained LOD values of 44.2, 212, 177 nmol L−1 for VB12, VB2, and VB6, respectively, using high-performance liquid chromatography with ultraviolet detection (HPLC-UV). Thus, the N,S-GQD-CS/GCE can be adopted as an alternative to chromatographic methods as an analytical tool, with superior sensitivity. In addition, the electrode modified with N,S-GQD-CS can be obtained using abundant low-cost materials and does not require sophisticated equipment for the synthesis of materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Repeatability, reproducibility, stability and selectivity studies
ce:section.ce:para: The precision of the analytical method developed for the determination of vitamins using the N,S-GQD-CS/GCE was evaluated by measuring the repeatability of the current response. This was carried out in 0.1 mol L−1 sodium acetate buffer (pH 5.0) containing 0.5 μmol L−1 VB2, 1.0 μmol L−1 VB12 and 2.0 μmol L−1 VB6 at the N,S-GQD-CS/GCE under optimized conditions. The values for the relative standard deviation (RSD) of the current measured on the same day with different films (intra-day repeatability) (n = 5) were 2.37% for VB12, 0.79% for VB2 and 3.44% for VB6, indicating good precision for the analytical signal obtained with the modified electrodes. The inter-day repeatability of the electrode responses was evaluated through five current measurements taken on five consecutive days, and the RSD values obtained were 4.32% for VB12, 3.34% for VB2 and 5.91% for VB6, indicating that, over the course of five days, a satisfactory precision in the measurements was maintained.

The reproducibility of the results is obtained by the analysis of the same sample under a variety of normal test conditions, such as different laboratories, analysts, and different lots of chemicals [46]. The reproducibility was examined by measuring the intensity current of 0.5 μmol L−1 VB2, 1.0 μmol L−1 VB12 and 2.0 μmol L−1 VB6 by SWV under optimized conditions in two laboratories with different potentiostats: Palmsens 4 (1) and PGSTAT101 (Metrohm Autolab B·V., The Netherlands) (2). RSD values of 4.70% (VB2), 6.15% (VB12) and 5.90% (VB6) were obtained from 10 current measurements, five in each lab, showing that the analytical method was reproductive.

The stability of the N,S-GQD-CS dispersion was investigated over a four-week period. The current responses provided by N,S-GQD-CS/GCE with the same dispersion for the three vitamins decreased about 6% after four weeks when compared with the first current response, indicating that the N,S-GQD-CS dispersion remained stable for the electrochemical application during this time.

To evaluate the selectivity provided by the N,S-GQD-CS/GCE in the presence of possible interference from organic molecules, assays were performed for the vitamins by SWASV. The tests were carried out in 0.1 mol L−1 sodium acetate buffer (pH 5.0) containing 0.5 μmol L−1 VB2 (−0.31 V vs. Ag/AgCl, KCl(sat)), 1.0 μmol L−1 VB12 (−0.77 V) and 2.0 μmol L−1 VB6 (+0.91 V) in the presence of potential interferents, which were added at a concentration 50 times higher than that of the analytes. The samples of energy drinks investigated in this study (Section 2.5) contained in their composition niacin (VB3), pantothenic acid (VB5), VB2, VB12, VB6, caffeine and taurine. Pantothenic acid and niacin showed no oxidation reaction. Assays with caffeine showed an oxidation peak at +1.2 V of low intensity. This observation is in agreement with previous studies in which the oxidation of caffeine was more favorable in more acidic media [51]. Thus, the presence of caffeine did not affect the oxidation of VB6 under the optimized conditions. Taurine not present an oxidation peak in the assays performed. In fact, taurine does not exhibit a significant electroactivity and derivatization steps are necessary to produce an electrochemically active species to enable electroanalysis to be performed [52]. These results indicate that the proposed N,S-GQD-CS/GCE had good selectivity toward VB2, VB6 and VB12 in energy drink samples containing B-group vitamins.

The N,S-GQD-CS/GCE was applied in the determination of VB2, VB6 and VB12 in two samples: fruit-based energy drink and classic energy drink (Table 2 ). To minimize the matrix effects, the standard addition method was used in the quantification assays. The results for one of the replicates in the determination of vitamins in the fruit-based energy drink sample are shown in Fig. S9. The square wave voltammogram obtained for the sample (Figs. S8A and S8B – curve b) show oxidation peaks for VB12, VB2 and VB6 at −0.76, −0.26 and + 0.95 V vs. Ag/AgCl, KCl(sat), respectively, which increased in current intensity with successive additions of standard solutions of the vitamins (Fig. S9A and S9B – curves c-f). The slopes of the standard addition plots (Fig. S9C-D – curve b) were similar to those of the calibration plots (Fig. S9C-D – curve a), which indicated that there was no interference from the matrix components in the determination of vitamins.

The accuracy was evaluated by conducting recovery tests (Table 2). Percentages in the range of 89–115, 92–107 and 90–110% were obtained for VB12, VB2 and VB6, respectively. According to the literature, acceptable recovery percentages are in the range of 80–110% for analyte concentrations at the micromolar level (VB2 and VB6) and in the range of 80–120% for analyte concentrations at the nanomolar level (VB12) [53]. These results verify that the N,S-GQD-CS/GCE is suitable for the determination of vitamins, providing satisfactory accuracy. In addition, relative errors below 3.0% were obtained compared to the values provided by the manufacturer on the product label.

The vitamin contents determined in the assays are in accordance with the limits established by the Brazilian regulatory agency. The regulation of energy drinks in Brazil by ANVISA (RDC 273/2005) allows the addition of 100% of the Recommended Daily Allowance (RDA) of vitamins in one portion of the product [54]. The RDA values are a maximum of 1.3 mg of VB2, 1.3 mg of VB6 (each) and 2.4 μg of VB12 for adults (RDC 269/2005) [55]. Thus, the analyzed energy drinks of company A contained 100% of the RDA for VB2 and VB6 and 42% for VB12, in agreement with the information on the product label (one portion of 250 mL). For the energy drinks of company B, the samples contained 100% of the RDA for the three vitamins, in agreement with the information on the product label (one portion of 200 mL). Once again, it should be noted that this study does not make any recommendation for the consumption of energy drinks. Our objective was solely to develop an analytical method for the determination of vitamins in these beverages, which was successfully performed.

An electrochemical method using an N,S-GQD–CS–based electrode was developed and applied in the simultaneous and sequential determination of vitamins in energy drinks. The presence of N,S-GQDs facilitated charge transfer in the system, providing higher current intensities and lower resistance to charge transfer when compared to the bare GCE. The chitosan also contributed to an increase in current responses through the adsorption of species, and it was fundamental for the formation of a stable film on the glassy carbon substrate. The sensitivity obtained by the modified electrode was sufficient to allow the quantification of vitamins at nanomolar levels. The recovery tests confirmed the accuracy of the data obtained by the modified electrode. Thus, the N,S-GQD-CS/GCE electrode can be applied as an analytical tool in the quality control of energy drinks providing sensitive, precise and accurate data.
